content.text:
INVESTIGATION OUTCOME: premise invalidated on implementation. A blanket fail-closed on unresolved blockers broke test_graph_archived_ticket_treated_as_closed — a depends_on whose target dir is absent is DELIBERATELY treated as closed (tombstone-awareness; an archived/deleted blocker must not block forever). resolve_ticket_id returns None for both normal archival and a bogus alias indistinguishably, so there is no clean fail-closed signal. Reverted the readiness changes; delivered instead: a documentation comment in process_link, a boundary+controls regression test, and 'safe broad catch' annotations on gates.py:400 + graph/_graph.py:250. No behavior change. Revising the AC to the honest delivered scope. Commit 974d40a4.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->
## Why
The 2026-06-18 audit (H-5) flagged `reducer/_processors.py` `process_link` as
storing a non-canonical `target_id` when resolution fails, claiming an
unresolvable-alias `depends_on` blocker would let a blocked ticket wrongly read as
`ready`. This story investigated and (where genuine) hardened the answer-affecting
broad-except sites.
## What
Investigate the claimed wrong-answer; determine whether it is a bug or intended
behavior; document the conclusion in code; add a regression/boundary test; and
annotate the remaining (benign) broad-except sites so future audits don't re-flag
them.
## Investigation outcome (premise REVISED)
The premise did **not** survive implementation. A blanket fail-closed (flag
unresolved deps; treat them as unsatisfied in the readiness paths) was implemented
and immediately broke `test_graph_archived_ticket_treated_as_closed`: a `depends_on`
whose target directory is **absent** is **deliberately** treated as closed → the
dependent is ready. This *tombstone-awareness* (an archived/deleted blocker must not
block its dependents forever) is the tested, intended invariant
(`_status._get_ticket_status`). Decisively, `resolve_ticket_id` returns `None` for
**both** normal archival **and** a genuinely-bogus alias, indistinguishably — so
there is no clean signal to fail closed on without breaking archived-blocker
unblocking. The fail-closed changes were reverted; readiness semantics are unchanged
(and were correct).
## Scope
- `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` (`process_link` — documentation comment only).
- `tests/scripts/graph/test_graph_unresolved_blocker.py` (new boundary + control tests).
- `src/rebar/_engine_support/gates.py:400`, `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py:250` (annotate the benign broad-except sites).
- Out of scope: any change to readiness semantics (they are the intended tombstone behavior).
## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] The claimed wrong-answer is investigated; conclusion is that the missing-target
`depends_on` readiness behavior is the **deliberate, tested tombstone invariant**,
not a bug.
- [x] `process_link` carries a comment documenting the boundary so the site is not
re-flagged by future audits.
- [x] A regression test codifies the boundary (absent target → ready) plus controls
(resolvable-open → blocked; resolvable-closed → ready).
- [x] `gates.py:400` and `graph/_graph.py:250` carry "safe broad catch" annotations
(validated benign — cosmetic summary / fail-open cycle guard).
- [x] No behavior change; ruff + the graph/reducer/lifecycle suites stay green.
</content>

content.text: ## What
Replace the destructive `git reset --hard origin/tickets` in the unrelated-histories
path of `_do_reconverge` with a non-destructive union merge, so local-only ticket
commits are never orphaned into the reflog. This makes the reflog non-load-bearing
and is what makes stock gc (WU-1) safe by construction.
## Scope (files)
- `src/rebar/_store/sync.py`: in `_do_reconverge`, Path 1 (no merge-base / unrelated
histories, ~71-85) replace `reset --hard origin/tickets` with
`git merge --allow-unrelated-histories origin/tickets --no-edit`, **reusing the
existing diverged-Path-3 conflict handling** (`merge --abort` -> keep local ->
hint `fsck`). Do not invent new conflict handling — extend the proven Path-3 code.
- Keep `reset --hard` ONLY for the ff-adopt path (`local_ahead` empty, ~90) which
discards nothing.
- Update the module docstring summary line that currently asserts
"unrelated histories -> reset --hard (never a merge)".
## Depends on
WU-3 (`.gitattributes merge=ours`) so the union merge converges on `bindings.json`
instead of conflicting.
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Path 1 uses `git merge --allow-unrelated-histories origin/tickets --no-edit`
- [ ] Conflict handling reuses Path 3 (`merge --abort`, keep local, hint fsck)
- [ ] ff-adopt path (`reset --hard`) unchanged; no orphaning `reset --hard` remains
- [ ] Test: union recovery from unrelated histories loses no local tickets
- [ ] Full non-integration suite green

content.text: ## What
Running a workflow against a **non-existent target ticket id** silently creates a
phantom, CREATE-less ticket directory in the store. The workflow recorder appends
`WORKFLOW_RUN`/`WORKFLOW_STEP` events to the target ticket's directory **without first
verifying the ticket exists**, and the store's commit path does
`os.makedirs(ticket_dir, exist_ok=True)` — so the events land in a brand-new directory
that has no `CREATE` event. The result is a "ghost" ticket that `fsck` flags as
`MISSING_CREATE` forever (it is excluded from `list` as an error-state, so it is easy to
miss until a store-health check trips on it).
This was discovered during the 0.5.2 release dogfood: an orphan dir
`31c6-d82c-44a5-4953` held a single `_fake: true` `WORKFLOW_STEP` (a FakeRunner run,
epic a88f) and no CREATE — it was the sole `MISSING_CREATE` fsck finding in the real
store (since cleaned up).
## Reproduction Steps
1. In an initialized store, run a workflow targeting a ticket id that does **not** exist,
e.g. library `rebar.run_workflow(<wf>, ticket_id="no-such-ticket")` (or the CLI/MCP
`run_workflow` equivalent), with the FakeRunner so no LLM is needed.
2. `rebar fsck`.
**Expected:** the run **fails fast** with a clear "ticket … not found / has no CREATE"
error and writes **no** event files — no directory is created for a bogus id (same
contract leaf writes already enforce).
**Actual:** the run proceeds and writes `WORKFLOW_RUN`/`WORKFLOW_STEP` events into a new
`no-such-ticket/` directory with no CREATE; `fsck` then reports
`MISSING_CREATE: no-such-ticket — no CREATE event found` permanently.
## Root cause (citations)
- `rebar.llm.workflow.runs.run_workflow` (`runs.py:169-192`) takes `ticket_id`, calls
`record_run_location`, and passes `target_ticket=ticket_id` to the executor **without
validating the ticket resolves / is not a ghost**.
- `executor.TicketEventRecorder._append` (`executor.py:233`) writes each
`WORKFLOW_RUN`/`WORKFLOW_STEP` via `_seam.append_event(self.ticket, …)`.
- `_seam.append_event` (`_commands/_seam.py:151-187`) composes the envelope and commits
through `_store.event_append.write_and_push` — it does **not** call
`_seam.require_not_ghost`, and `stage_and_commit` does `os.makedirs(ticket_dir,
exist_ok=True)`, so a non-existent target id materializes a CREATE-less dir.
- Contrast: the leaf-write commands (comment/edit/tag/…) call
`_seam.require_not_ghost(ticket_id, tracker)` (`_seam.py:70-83`) BEFORE appending,
precisely to refuse writing onto a ticket that was never created. The workflow
recorder is missing that guard.
## Proposed fix
Validate the target ticket up front, before the first `WORKFLOW_RUN` write:
- In `run_workflow` (the entry point), when `ticket_id` is provided, resolve it and run
the ghost check (`_seam.require_id` + `_seam.require_not_ghost`, or the library
equivalent) and fail fast with a clear error if it does not exist — BEFORE
`record_run_location` / starting the run, so no partial state is written.
- Optionally add the same `require_not_ghost` guard in `TicketEventRecorder` (or its
first `_append`) as defense-in-depth for any other caller that constructs a recorder
with a bogus target.
- Do not auto-create the ticket; recording run-state onto a non-existent ticket is a
caller error, mirroring the leaf-write contract.
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `run_workflow` (library + CLI + MCP) against a non-existent `ticket_id` fails fast
with a clear "ticket not found / has no CREATE" error and writes **zero** event files —
no phantom directory is created.
- [ ] `run_workflow` against a real, existing ticket records `WORKFLOW_RUN`/`WORKFLOW_STEP`
exactly as before (no regression to the happy path or to `dry_run`).
- [ ] `fsck` stays clean after running workflows (no new `MISSING_CREATE`).
- [ ] A regression test exercises the non-existent-ticket case (asserts the fail-fast
error AND that no `<bogus-id>/` directory was created).
</content>

REB-121.summary: workflow engine records WORKFLOW_RUN/STEP to a non-existent target ticket -> phantom CREATE-less dir (fsck MISSING_CREATE)

content.text:
Done (commit pending). execution_mode {single_turn,agentic} enum on prompt front-matter (default agentic, reject invalid); runner single_turn = one structured call, tools=[]/toolsets=[], validated vs prompt.outputs (build_agent_request dispatch seam; raises if no outputs); agentic unchanged. Inspector surfaces agent prompt contracts (ServiceTask ContractEntry, bundle rebuilt). prompt_override_drift flags override outputs drift via lint_workflow. 316 tests green incl offline single_turn + direct no-tools assertion + live external test (inert). Verified dispatch logic + no-tools branch directly.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

**Completion verification: PASS**

All 6 acceptance criteria are demonstrably met by the implementation.

**AC#1 — `execution_mode` enum + documentation**: `EXECUTION_MODES = ("single_turn", "agentic")` is defined in `src/rebar/llm/prompts.py:51` with a detailed docblock (lines 39–51) explicitly distinguishing it from step `mode`. The same distinction is documented in `src/rebar/llm/runner.py:44–50`.

**AC#2 — single_turn dispatch + agentic unchanged + tests**: `PydanticAIRunner.run()` at `runner.py:181–183` sets `tools=[]; toolsets=[]` for `single_turn`. `build_agent_request()` in `runs.py:179–201` overrides mode to `"structured"` + the prompt's `outputs` schema for `single_turn`, and leaves the step's own mode/schema for `agentic`. Covered by unit tests in `tests/unit/test_execution_mode.py` (dispatch + no-tools) and `tests/interfaces/store/test_execution_mode_dispatch.py` (end-to-end offline via FakeRunner, including the direct no-tools assertion at lines 57–111).

**AC#3 — Inspector surfaces agent prompt contracts**: `prompt_contract_view()` (`editor.py:183–207`) resolves a prompt's description/consumes/produces from front-matter; `resolve_contracts()` (lines 210–238) maps every agent step's `prompt` key to its contract view. Tested in `test_execution_mode.py:145–173`.

**AC#4 — `validate` flags override/variant outputs drift**: `prompt_override_drift()` (`prompts.py:590–629`) compares override vs built-in `outputs`; integrated into `lint_workflow()` via `lint.py:221,225–237` as `error`-severity findings. Tests in `test_execution_mode.py:184–228` cover changed outputs flagged, identical override clean, and surfacing via `lint_workflow`.

**AC#5 — Live single_turn produces structured output**: Live external test at `tests/external/test_execution_mode_live.py:26–46` (skipped without `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`/`REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1`); offline end-to-end demo in `test_execution_mode_dispatch.py:22–53` validates the structured output path via FakeRunner.

**AC#6 — Missing execution_mode defaults to agentic + closed enum + S3 migration**: `get_prompt()` defaults to `"agentic"` when absent (`prompts.py:193–194`) and raises `PromptError` for invalid values (lines 195–199). Both are tested in `test_execution_mode.py:22–60`. S3's migration stamped `execution_mode: agentic` explicitly on all 6 reviewers, confirmed in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` and individual `.md` files (e.g., `code_quality.md:6`).

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->
## Why
Make prompts contract-bearing palette citizens like scripted ops, and represent the single_turn
vs agentic distinction precisely so the runner can be written without inventing protocol.
## What
- `execution_mode` is a CLOSED enum `{single_turn, agentic}` on the PROMPT front-matter (NOT the
workflow IR; it is DISTINCT from the existing step `mode:[findings,structured,text]`, which controls
output shaping and is unchanged). Document the relationship between the two fields.
- Runner DISPATCH protocol (`src/rebar/llm/runner.py` + `src/rebar/llm/workflow/runs.py`
RunnerAgentStep): `agentic` = the current pydantic_ai tool-using path (unchanged). `single_turn` =
ONE `pydantic_ai` model call with NO tools; the resolved prompt is rendered with the step's `with:`
inputs marshaled as template variables; the model is asked for structured output validated against
the prompt's `outputs` contract (reuse the existing structured-output stack); no tool loop.
- Surface the resolved prompt's consumes/produces/description in the inspector (reuse S0's path).
- `outputs` contract change on an override/variant is BREAKING: `validate` flags override-vs-builtin drift.
## Scope
In: enum + runner dispatch (both modes) + prompt-contract surfacing + drift flag. Out: deep/runtime validation (S5).
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `execution_mode` is a `{single_turn,agentic}` enum on prompt front-matter, documented as distinct from step `mode`.

- [ ] `single_turn` runs exactly one model call with zero tools and returns output validated against the prompt's `outputs` contract; `agentic` is the unchanged tool-using path; both covered by tests.
- [ ] An agent step surfaces its prompt's contract in the inspector.
- [ ] `validate` flags an override/variant that changes a prompt's `outputs` contract.
- [ ] A live `single_turn` prompt produces structured output validated against its `outputs` contract.

- [ ] A prompt that LACKS `execution_mode` defaults to `agentic` (back-compat) — tested; the field is otherwise the closed `{single_turn,agentic}` enum, and S3's migration stamps it explicitly on existing prompts.

content.text:
**Completion verification result: PASS**

All three acceptance criteria are satisfied by the implementation in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/`.

**AC1 — Sign + material-commit bind + invalidation-on-material-edit; coverage recorded.**
- HMAC-SHA256 signing: `attest.sign_plan_review()` (line 56–62) calls `signing.sign_manifest()`, which embeds `head_sha` (git-commit binding) and the HMAC under the environment key.
- Material fingerprint: `orchestrator.material_fingerprint()` (lines 701–712) hashes `ticket_id + description + file_impact + children` with SHA-256; explicitly excludes tags/comments/links/assignee.
- The fingerprint is bound into the signed manifest (`attest.build_manifest`, line 38: `f"material: {material}"`).
- Invalidation: `claim_gate_check()` (`attest.py` lines 106–123) recomputes the fingerprint at claim time and blocks with `stale-material` if it changed.
- Coverage: assembled in the orchestrator (`coverage` dict covering DET-floor per-check results, routing, LLM call counts, blocking/advisory counts); recorded in the schema (`plan_review_verdict.schema.json` line 46–50) and in the signed manifest (`blocking`/`advisory` lines in `build_manifest`).

**AC2 — Externally CI-checkable (signed result + coverage).**
- `rebar review-plan <id>` CLI (`_cli/__init__.py` lines 290–335) exits 0 (PASS) / 1 (BLOCK) / 2 (INDETERMINATE) and emits JSON (with `--output json`) that includes both `signature` and `coverage` fields, making it scriptable as a CI step. Documented in `plan-review-gate.md` lines 31–32.

**AC3 — Reuses the existing HMAC signing, no new key custody.**
- `attest.py` lines 3–10 document the reuse explicitly; `sign_plan_review()` calls `rebar.signing.sign_manifest()` verbatim — the same function used by the close gate. No new key material, no new custody mechanism.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

PASS-1 REVIEW — criterion T1 (Prior-art / novel-architecture justification) [overlay]

VERDICT: clean — no findings.

A security/signing bright-line fires, so T1 applies. The plan satisfies it on all three sub-checks:

AFFIRMATIONS (criteria passed):
- T1(a) Prior art considered / not reinventing: The What/Scope explicitly reuse the existing close-gate signing machinery ("reuse the close-gate signing machinery"; "Reuse the close-gate signing; no new key custody"). This is grounded in the live surface — `docs/reuse-surface.md` §1 ("HMAC-SHA256 attestation over a ticket's manifest of verified steps … reuse it for any 'this was verified' attestation. No new key custody is ever needed") and `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` ("reuses the close-gate signing machinery verbatim … no new key custody"). The plan builds on existing prior art rather than reinventing it.
- T1(b) Novel-pattern justification: no novel architecture is introduced. HMAC-SHA256 is a standard primitive; the material-fingerprint binding is layered on the established head_sha freshness-binding pattern already in `rebar.signing` / `attest.build_manifest`. No anti-repackaging / Rule-of-Three concern.
- T1(c) Unverified capability assertions resolved: the load-bearing reuse claims ("reuses existing HMAC signing, no new key custody"; "integrity/forgery/replay/staleness model inherited") are verified against `docs/reuse-surface.md` and the implemented `attest.py` — not unresolved assertions.

A well-specified plan that reuses an established, in-repo primitive is a PASS for T1.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

PASS-1 REVIEW (criterion G6 — approach soundness, anti-patterns & alternative-selection)

AFFIRMATIONS (criteria this chunk passes):
- G6 FITNESS-FOR-PURPOSE: the approach (HMAC-sign the review result + invalidate on material edit + record coverage) directly produces the named "composable CI rigor / anti-vibe-coding signal" — it solves the real problem, not a proxy. Confirmed implementable against the existing surface (`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py`, `src/rebar/signing.py`).
- G6 APPROACH SELECTION: the chosen approach is defensibly at-least-as-good as the obvious structural alternative (bind invalidation to a git-commit SHA only). A code-commit binding alone would miss ticket-content edits that produce no commit; the plan's content-fingerprint-of-material approach is the more robust choice and aligns with what the codebase already does (`orchestrator.material_fingerprint`). No superior missed alternative — discarded.
- G6 POSITIVE RATIONALE: present and adequate — "reuse the close-gate signing machinery; no new key custody; integrity/forgery/replay/staleness model inherited" states why this approach fits. PASS (no rejected-alternatives section required).

FINDINGS (coaching, not blockers):

- [ ] Reconcile the stated binding mechanism with the actual two-part binding, so the implementer builds the invalidation that works. (criteria: G6 / mechanism correctness) — location: "What" bullet, phrase "bind to the latest MATERIAL event commit". Evidence: the sound, in-code mechanism is NOT a "material event commit"; it is TWO separate bindings — (1) a `head_sha` code-HEAD git binding (audit/freshness; `attest.claim_gate_check` lines 95–102) and (2) a SHA-256 **content fingerprint** of `{ticket_id, description, file_impact, children}` hashed into the signed manifest (`orchestrator.material_fingerprint` lines 723–734, bound via `attest.build_manifest` line 36). It is the content fingerprint — not any "commit" — that invalidates on material edits. The plan's "latest MATERIAL event commit" phrasing risks an implementer wiring invalidation to a commit SHA, which would NOT detect a ticket-only material edit (description/AC change with no new code commit). suggested_fix: reword to "bind a content fingerprint of the material fields (description/AC/file_impact/decomposition) into the signed manifest; additionally record code-HEAD (`head_sha`) for freshness — invalidate the attestation when EITHER changes."

- [ ] State how "AC" and "decomposition" map onto the fingerprint basis, since the current basis hashes neither directly. (criteria: G6 / mechanism correctness, edge-case completeness) — location: "What" bullet, "invalidate on material edits (description/AC/file_impact/decomposition…)". Evidence: `material_fingerprint` (orchestrator.py 727–732) hashes `description`, `file_impact`, and `children` (child IDs, sorted) — it does NOT hash an `AC` field separately (AC lives inside `description` here) and it captures decomposition only as the SET of child ids, so an edit to a CHILD's own material content (re-decomposition of a child's body) would not flip the parent fingerprint. The plan asserts all four fields are covered; clarify whether that is exact (AC⊆description; decomposition = child-id set) so the AC's invalidation claim is verifiable and not over-promised. suggested_fix: (empty — author should confirm intended granularity before wording.)

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

PASS-1 REVIEW — criterion T3 (Integration feasibility) [overlay]

VERDICT: clean — no findings (not-applicable / PASS).

T3 is an overlay that applies only when the plan integrates an EXTERNAL API/CLI/service/library or asserts a capability it has not used before. This plan does neither:

AFFIRMATIONS (criteria passed):
- T3(a) technical_feasibility: The integration is achievable as described and reuses an EXISTING in-repo primitive. "HMAC-sign the review result under the MCP environment key (reuse the close-gate signing machinery)" maps directly to `rebar.signing.sign_manifest()` / `compute_signature()` (src/rebar/signing.py:203, ALGORITHM = "HMAC-SHA256" at :50), already called verbatim by `attest.sign_plan_review()` (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:56-62). No capability gap.
- T3(b) named-capabilities MATCH: the asserted reuse surface exists and matches — material-commit/head_sha binding and material-edit invalidation are already implemented (`attest.claim_gate_check`, `orchestrator.material_fingerprint`), and the reuse contract is documented (docs/reuse-surface.md §1, docs/event-schema.md SIGNATURE row). This is an internal, already-used integration → not a MISMATCH/UNVERIFIED external dependency.
- T3(c) auth/HTTPS preconditions: not-applicable — no network/external service. The claim path is explicitly "no-llm/no-network … a pure local HMAC verify" (attest.py:73; orchestrator.py:580). Key custody is inherited, not new ("no new key custody").
- T3(d) SPIKE routing: not required — there is no critical capability gap; the primitive is established in-repo.

Per T3's ANTI-FP rule ("an internal, already-used integration is not-applicable"), this chunk is a clean PASS for T3. (Note: mechanism-correctness nuances around the "MATERIAL event commit" phrasing vs. the actual content-fingerprint binding are real, but those are G6/mechanism findings already surfaced — not a T3 external-integration feasibility gap.)

The chosen mechanism (reuse signing.sign_manifest / the SIGNATURE event under the env HMAC key, bind a material fingerprint into the signed manifest, inherit code-HEAD freshness, exclude tags/comments/links/assignee from invalidation) matches the implemented design in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py and docs/plan-review-gate.md §Attestation; mechanism is correct and fit-for-purpose. The chosen approach — reuse the close-gate HMAC-SHA256 signing, bind the material fingerprint INTO the signed manifest (so HMAC protects it), invalidate by recompute-and-compare plus inherited code-HEAD freshness — is logic-complete, has no TOCTOU (the fingerprint is inside the signed payload), fails closed on an unrecoverable fingerprint, and converges LWW on concurrent signs. Attestation (attest.py): build_manifest ('plan-review: PASS' + material fingerprint), sign_plan_review (reuses rebar.signing.sign_manifest -> SIGNATURE event), claim_gate_check (certified + code-HEAD freshness + material-edit invalidation; fail-CLOSED on unverifiable). material_fingerprint binds description/AC/file_impact/decomposition. **Completion verification result: PASS**

All three acceptance criteria are satisfied by the implementation in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/`.

**AC1 — Sign + material-commit bind + invalidation-on-material-edit; coverage recorded.**
- HMAC-SHA256 signing: `attest.sign_plan_review()` (attest.py:56–62) calls `rebar.signing.sign_manifest()`, which embeds `head_sha` (git-commit binding) and the HMAC under the environment key (signing.py:319–359).
- Material fingerprint: `orchestrator.material_fingerprint()` (orchestrator.py:723–734) hashes `ticket_id + description + file_impact + children (sorted)` with SHA-256; explicitly excludes tags/comments/links/assignee.
- Fingerprint bound into the signed manifest: `build_manifest()` (attest.py:36) inserts `f"material: {material}"` so the HMAC covers it.
- Invalidation: `claim_gate_check()` (attest.py:106–123) recomputes the fingerprint at claim time and blocks with `stale-material` if it changed; fails closed if fingerprint can't be recomputed.
- Coverage: assembled in the orchestrator (orchestrator.py:467–592) — DET-floor per-check results, routing, LLM call counts, blocking/advisory counts — and recorded in the schema (plan_review_verdict.schema.json:46–50) and the signed manifest (attest.py:39–40). MET.

**AC2 — Externally CI-checkable (signed result + coverage).**
- `rebar review-plan <id>` CLI (_cli/__init__.py:290–335) exits 0 (PASS) / 1 (BLOCK) / 2 (INDETERMINATE) and emits JSON (default `--output json`) that includes both `signature` and `coverage` fields, making it scriptable as a CI step. MET.

**AC3 — Reuses the existing HMAC signing, no new key custody.**
- attest.py:3–10 documents the reuse explicitly; `sign_plan_review()` calls `rebar.signing.sign_manifest()` verbatim (attest.py:62) — the same function used by the close gate (transition.py:384). No new key material, no new custody mechanism. MET.

VERDICT: PASS

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

CONTAINER REVIEW — G4 (child consistency) — parent epic `5fd2` × child `4bb7` (Attestation: signing + material-commit binding + invalidation): NO FINDINGS (clean pairing).

Audited all 7 cross-child interaction modes for this child against the WHOLE sibling roster; high-confidence + benign-reading filter applied. Each mode resolves consistent-by-design.

1. IMPLICIT SHARED STATE — none unowned/double-written. 4bb7 shares three pieces of state with siblings, each with a single clean owner: (a) the MATERIAL FINGERPRINT is COMPUTED by `ca03` (`orchestrator.material_fingerprint()`, hashing description/AC/file_impact/decomposition); 4bb7 BINDS it into the signed manifest (`build_manifest` "material: {material}"); `092b` RECOMPUTES it at claim time to detect staleness; `db7b` RECORDS it in the sidecar payload. Compute=ca03 / bind=4bb7 / recompute-check=092b / record=db7b — four distinct verbs over one value, no double-write. (b) The signed manifest / SIGNATURE head-state event — 4bb7 produces it (reusing `signing.sign_manifest`), `092b` reads it (pure local HMAC verify). (c) "coverage recorded" — coverage content assembled by `ca03`, bound into the manifest counts by 4bb7, recorded for offline analysis by `db7b`. No conflicting shared state.

2. CONFLICTING ASSUMPTIONS — examined the most plausible candidate and it is NOT a cross-child contradiction. 4bb7's "What" says "bind to the latest MATERIAL event commit," whereas the actual binding (and what siblings `092b`/`db7b` consume) is a two-part scheme: a SHA-256 CONTENT FINGERPRINT of the material fields (invalidates on material edit) PLUS a `head_sha` code-HEAD git binding (freshness). This wording was already surfaced as a within-child G6 mechanism-correctness coaching finding on this ticket (the "material event commit" phrase risks wiring invalidation to a commit SHA). But it is NOT a G4 conflict: the siblings agree on the real mechanism — `092b`'s `claim_gate_check` recomputes the content fingerprint (not a commit) and `db7b` records `material_fingerprint` — so there is no contradictory ASSUMPTION held across children. The material set is stated IDENTICALLY everywhere (description/AC/file_impact/decomposition invalidate; tags/comments/links/assignee do not) in the parent, 4bb7, 092b, and db7b. Benign-reading filter: PASS (a phrasing imprecision in this child's own prose, not a sibling-level inconsistency).

3. DEPENDENCY GAP — none. Consume/produce edges are coherent: 4bb7 PRODUCES the signed attestation + material binding; `092b` (claim) CONSUMES it (fast verify) and is correctly sequenced AFTER 4bb7 in the parent's risk-ordered MVP spine; `db7b` records the same fingerprint. The material_fingerprint 4bb7 binds is computed by `ca03`, which is also in the MVP slice ahead of/with attestation. No missing sequencing edge.

4. SCOPE OVERLAP — none. 4bb7's slice (HMAC-sign the verdict reusing the close-gate machinery; bind the material fingerprint INTO the manifest; invalidate on material edit; bind coverage counts; no new key custody) does not double-own any sibling concern: fingerprint COMPUTATION is ca03's, the claim-path VERIFY + --force audit + fail-closed-on-signing-unavailable is 092b's, the sidecar RECORDING of material_fingerprint/coverage is db7b's. 4bb7 owns the signing/binding/invalidation verbs only. No double-ownership.

5. ORDERING VIOLATION — none. 4bb7 is in the parent's stated thin vertical slice (DET floor 012e + registry/orchestrator ca03 + ONE single-turn criterion + Pass-1/2/3 + attestation 4bb7 + claim 092b), correctly placed before 092b which depends on a signed attestation existing. Correct dependency order.

6. CONSUMER IMPACT — none. 4bb7's downstream consumers do not contradict its shape: `092b` recomputes the exact same fingerprint basis and blocks with `stale-material` — consistent with 4bb7's invalidation contract; the signing-infra-UNAVAILABLE fail-CLOSED posture is owned by the consumer `092b` (added AC) and is consistent with 4bb7's "integrity/forgery/replay/staleness model inherited" (the close gate is fail-closed); `db7b` records the fingerprint read-only. The "material vs non-material" edit partition is stated identically across all consumers — no drift.

7. RESIDUAL REFERENCES — none. 4bb7 references only LIVE artifacts: "the close-gate signing machinery" (`rebar.signing.sign_manifest`, the same function the close gate uses) and "the MCP environment key" (inherited key custody) — both resolve to real, named in-repo surfaces. No removed/renamed sibling artifact is referenced. (The "MATERIAL event commit" phrase is a wording imprecision, not a dangling reference to a non-existent sibling.)

NET: clean for G4 — this child is consistent with the parent and every sibling it interacts with (ca03 / 092b / db7b) across all 7 modes. The single examined near-candidate (the "latest MATERIAL event commit" phrasing vs. the actual content-fingerprint + head_sha binding) is a within-child G6 mechanism-correctness coaching point already recorded on this ticket, NOT a cross-child G4 contradiction — siblings consistently implement and consume the real two-part binding. Findings list is EMPTY.

## Why
The signed result is the composable CI rigor / anti-vibe-coding signal.
## What
- HMAC-sign the review result under the MCP environment key (reuse the close-gate signing machinery); bind to
the latest MATERIAL event commit; invalidate on material edits (description/AC/file_impact/decomposition;
not tags/comments/links/assignee); record coverage.
## Scope
- Reuse the close-gate signing; no new key custody. Integrity/forgery/replay/staleness model inherited.
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Sign + material-commit bind + invalidation-on-material-edit; coverage recorded.
- [ ] Externally CI-checkable (signed result + coverage).
- [ ] Reuses the existing HMAC signing, no new key custody.

content.text: ## What
Add a repo-wide guard so future tests that are improperly isolated fail loudly instead of silently polluting the checkout (the failure mode behind jinx-event-gram).

Files: tests/conftest.py (per-test HEAD guard + session working-tree backstop), tests/_isolation.py (shared detection primitives: head/porcelain), tests/unit/test_repo_isolation_guard.py (pytester self-test proving the guard fires).
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A per-test autouse fixture fails any test that moves the repo HEAD (commit leak), with an opt-out marker allow_repo_writes
- [ ] A session backstop fails the run if git status --porcelain gains net-new entries (working-tree writes beyond top-level)
- [ ] A self-test proves the guard fires on a committing test and a working-tree write, and passes a clean test
- [ ] No false positives across the unit + scripts tiers

content.text:
Partially delivered as part of the 744b editor round-trip: layered layout (no overlap), edge waypoints, expanded sub-processes, working properties panel (kind/action/prompt-text/condition/config), connection labels (true/false/default/after), start/end events. REMAINING UX + architecture (prompt library/dropdown, MCP steps, input/output contracts, single-turn vs agentic, editable prompts, in-editor JSON validation/help) will be folded into the upcoming workflow-editing remediation epic (to be designed via /joe-brainstorm). Keeping OPEN until re-parented there.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

Already DELIVERED before this epic's execution by commit 2c66b1c7 'real layered layout in the serializer (no overlaps, docked edges, expanded sub-processes)'. bpmn.py is byte-identical between main and the epic branch. Verified all ACs on roundtrip-demo.yaml: 20 shapes at distinct positions (no overlap), 5 expanded sub-processes (loop/map/branch bodies inline), 15 edges all with >=2 docked waypoints (source right edge -> target left edge). Pinned by the existing test test_layout_has_no_overlaps_edges_and_expanded_subprocesses (asserts no-overlap + edges + expanded children inside parent). No new code required.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

**Completion verification — PASS**

All four acceptance criteria are met by the implementation in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/bpmn.py`, pinned by `tests/unit/workflow/test_bpmn.py::test_layout_has_no_overlaps_edges_and_expanded_subprocesses`.

**AC1 — No two nodes overlap; left-to-right with parallel siblings on distinct rows.**
`_layout_frame` (bpmn.py:369–441) performs a longest-path rank assignment: each node's x comes from its rank column, and same-rank siblings are stacked vertically with `y += h + _VGAP` (line 431). The test asserts `len(set(tops)) == len(tops)` (line 456) — no two shapes share the same top-left position.

**AC2 — Arrows dock to node edges (no center-through-text routing).**
`_emit_di` (bpmn.py:444–475) emits the source waypoint at `sx + sw + _MARGIN` (right edge of source) and target waypoint at `tx + _MARGIN` (left edge of target), explicitly not center-to-center. Docstring confirms: "docked to the source's right edge and the target's left edge (not centre-to-centre, so arrows don't cut through node labels)."

**AC3 — loop/map/branch bodies visible as expanded sub-processes.**
`_layout_frame` calls `expanded.add(sid)` for loop, map, and branch-arm sub-processes (lines 393, 403). `_emit_di` sets `isExpanded="true"` for any sid in `expanded` (line 460). The test asserts `"refine"` (loop) and `"fanout"` (map) are in expanded, and that child `"attempt"` coordinates satisfy containment within `"refine"` bounds (lines 460–463).

**AC4 — roundtrip-demo.yaml opens legibly (layout + edges + nesting).**
`tests/e2e/fixtures/roundtrip-demo.yaml` exists and exercises all constructs (scripted steps, loop, map, branch, nested branch). The `ir_to_bpmn` pipeline applies the same `_layout_frame` + `_emit_di` path to any IR including this file. The unit test validates the concrete DI output using `_full_v2()`, a comprehensive equivalent fixture covering all control constructs.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->
## Why
The 744b editor renders structural topology with a naive layout — a broken viewer, not a legible
view. This story makes the canvas LEGIBLE (layout/edges/nesting). NOTE (post plan-review reconciliation):
the PROPERTIES PANEL is owned by S8 (a83a-fadf-d7f2-43da), and BRANCH/CONNECTION UX is deferred (epic S9) —
both REMOVED from this story to eliminate the overlap the review flagged.
## What
1. **Real layered layout.** `_emit_di` sets y from nesting-lane only, so top-level siblings share y=80
and overlap. Generate a proper layered layout (rank=x, sibling order=y) — own Python layout, NOT
bpmn-auto-layout (it stacks our event-free nested IR in one column; see the 744b session log).
2. **Edge waypoints.** We emit 2 waypoints at shape CENTERS; arrows cross node text. Emit cropped/docked
waypoints so arrows dock to node edges on open.
3. **Inline nesting.** Child shapes land on the root plane with no `isExpanded`/containment, so loop/map/
branch bodies open in a separate drill-down and overlap. Emit EXPANDED sub-processes (isExpanded=true +
child bounds inside parent) so nesting shows on one canvas (or make drill-down deliberate + documented).
## Scope
Editor host (`editor.py build_host_html`) + DI generation (`bpmn.py _emit_di`/`_emit_step`). OUT: properties
panel (S8), branch/connection UX (deferred S9), IR semantics. Uses the already-vendored bpmn-js bundle.
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No two nodes overlap on open; the top frame reads left-to-right with parallel siblings on distinct rows.
- [ ] Arrows dock to node edges on open (no center-through-text routing).
- [ ] loop/map/branch bodies are visible without leaving the canvas (expanded sub-processes), or drill-down is intentional + documented.
- [ ] roundtrip-demo.yaml opens legibly and demonstrates layout + edges + nesting (properties/branch UX are out of scope, owned by S8 / deferred).

content.text:
## Completion Verification Result: PASS

All acceptance criteria are met by the implementation. Here is the criterion-by-criterion assessment:

---

### AC1 — Claim blocks on absent/material-edit-invalidated signature; fast check meets latency target.
**MET.** `_plan_review_precheck` (transition.py) calls `claim_gate_check` (llm/plan_review/attest.py), which is a pure local HMAC verify + fingerprint recompute — no LLM, no network. Code comments explicitly reference the ~50ms target. Tests: `test_gate_on_blocks_claim_without_attestation` and `test_material_edit_invalidates_attestation` in `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py`.

### AC2 — --force override is available and logged with justification.
**MET.** `_parse_force` in transition.py parses `--force[=<reason>]`; `_plan_review_precheck` appends a `FORCE_CLAIM: …` comment (via `leaf.comment`) with the justification before bypassing. A bare `--force` yields `"(no reason given)"` so the bypass is always audit-logged. Test: `test_force_bypasses_gate_with_audit`.

### AC3 — No LLM/network on the claim path.
**MET.** Structurally enforced: the `claim_gate_check` function imports `signing` (pure local HMAC), not the LLM stack. Test: `test_claim_path_makes_no_llm_call` monkey-patches both `rebar.llm.review_plan` and `rebar.llm.verify_completion` to raise, then confirms `rebar.claim` still succeeds (pure local verify).

### AC4 (G3) — End-to-end proving check covering all named edge cases.
**MET.** The full loop (claim-block → review-plan → earn signature → claim succeeds) is tested by `test_review_then_claim_succeeds`. Each named edge case is covered: `--force` + logging (`test_force_bypasses_gate_with_audit`), material-edit invalidation (`test_material_edit_invalidates_attestation`), gate-off preference (`test_gate_off_by_default_claims_without_attestation`), DET-floor block → no signature → claim blocked (`test_det_block_yields_no_signature`). The "fail-open on unsupported stack" is tested at unit level (`test_det_floor_fails_open_on_check_error`); "cap-hit INDETERMINATE" is handled by the same "no signed PASS → `claim_gate_check` returns `ok: False` → claim blocked" path that IS E2E-tested.

### AC5 — ENFORCE config preference (defaults OFF).
**MET.** `config.py` declares `require_plan_review_for_claim: bool = False`; `_plan_review_precheck` reads this and returns `None` immediately when `off`. Tests: `test_gate_off_by_default_claims_without_attestation`, `test_gate_on_blocks_claim_without_attestation`, and `test_config_flag_default_off`.

### AC6 (T9) — Signature-verify + open→in_progress are ONE atomic operation.
**MET.** `claim_compute` runs `_plan_review_precheck` (the fast verify, outside the lock by design), then `txn.claim_core` acquires the unified write lock, re-reads the ticket status (optimistic-concurrency: exit 10 if not `open`), writes STATUS + EDIT events, and commits them in a SINGLE `git commit` before releasing the lock. No check-then-act gap within the locked section; concurrent double-claims resolve via exit 10 per rebar's replay semantics.

### AC7 (signing-infra unavailability) — Claim fails closed when gate is ENABLED and signing machinery is unavailable.
**MET.** `claim_gate_check` (attest.py line 77–80) wraps `signing.verify_signature` in a `try/except Exception` and returns `{ok: False, reason: "signing-unavailable: …"}` on any exception — which `_plan_review_precheck` converts to a blocking `CommandError`. When the gate is DISABLED (`off`), `_plan_review_precheck` returns `None` before reaching the signature check. `--force` remains available as the escape and is audit-logged.

---

**Summary:** The implementation in `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py`, `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py`, `src/rebar/_commands/txn.py`, `src/rebar/signing.py`, and `src/rebar/config.py` satisfies every stated acceptance criterion. The test suite in `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py` and `tests/unit/test_plan_review.py` provides the required E2E and unit-level coverage.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

Claim integration: transition._plan_review_precheck wired into claim_compute (OUTSIDE the lock; fail-open unreadable config; bugs/session_logs exempt) + claim --force=<reason> (audit-logged). config verify.require_plan_review_for_claim (default OFF; threaded via rebar.claim(force=)). Claim path makes NO LLM/network call (pure local HMAC verify) — interface-tested. Fail-CLOSED when enabled + signing unavailable. Reuses the atomic claim primitive. verify-completion: PASS.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

## Completion Verification Result: PASS

Every acceptance criterion is met by the implementation. Criterion-by-criterion evidence:

---

### AC1 — Claim blocks on absent/material-edit-invalidated signature; fast check meets latency target.
**MET.** `_plan_review_precheck` (transition.py:634–695) calls `llm.claim_gate_check` (attest.py:66–124), which is a pure local HMAC verify + fingerprint recompute — no LLM, no network. The module docstring (attest.py:12–16) explicitly notes the ~50ms target. Material-edit invalidation is enforced at attest.py:106–123. Tests: `test_gate_on_blocks_claim_without_attestation` and `test_material_edit_invalidates_attestation` in `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py`.

### AC2 — --force override is available and logged with justification.
**MET.** `_parse_force` (transition.py:602–611) parses `--force[=<reason>]`; a bare `--force` yields `"(no reason given)"` so the bypass is always audit-logged. `_plan_review_precheck` (transition.py:668–681) writes `FORCE_CLAIM: …` via `leaf.comment` before bypassing. The public `rebar.claim(force=…)` API threads it through at `__init__.py:197`. Test: `test_force_bypasses_gate_with_audit`.

### AC3 — No LLM/network on the claim path.
**MET.** `claim_gate_check` (attest.py) imports only `rebar.signing` (pure local HMAC via stdlib `hmac`/`hashlib`), not the LLM stack. Test `test_claim_path_makes_no_llm_call` monkey-patches both `rebar.llm.review_plan` and `rebar.llm.verify_completion` to raise, then confirms `rebar.claim` succeeds.

### AC4 (G3) — End-to-end proving check + all named edge cases.
**MET.** Full loop (claim-block → review-plan → earn signature → claim succeeds) is `test_review_then_claim_succeeds`. Named edge cases: `--force` + logging (`test_force_bypasses_gate_with_audit`), material-edit invalidation (`test_material_edit_invalidates_attestation`), gate-off preference (`test_gate_off_by_default_claims_without_attestation`), DET-floor block → no signature → claim blocked (`test_det_block_yields_no_signature`). Fail-open on unsupported stack: `test_det_floor_fails_open_on_check_error`. Cap-hit INDETERMINATE path: covered by the same "no signed PASS → claim blocked" path in `test_gate_on_blocks_claim_without_attestation`.

### AC5 — ENFORCE config preference (defaults OFF).
**MET.** `config.py:260`: `require_plan_review_for_claim: bool = False`. `_plan_review_precheck` (transition.py:661–662) returns `None` immediately when `off`. Tests: `test_config_flag_default_off` (unit), `test_gate_off_by_default_claims_without_attestation`, and `test_gate_on_blocks_claim_without_attestation` cover both states.

### AC6 (T9) — Signature-verify + open→in_progress are ONE atomic operation.
**MET.** `_plan_review_precheck` runs OUTSIDE the lock (transition.py:720–726 with explicit comment). `txn.claim_core` (txn.py:331–435) then acquires the write lock, re-reads ticket status under lock, writes STATUS + EDIT events, and commits them in a SINGLE `git commit` (txn.py:422) before releasing. `ConcurrencyMismatch` (exit 10) on non-open status (txn.py:365–369).

### AC7 (signing-infra unavailability) — Claim fails closed when gate ENABLED + signing machinery unavailable.
**MET.** `claim_gate_check` (attest.py:77–80) wraps `signing.verify_signature` in `try/except Exception` and returns `{ok: False, reason: "signing-unavailable: …"}` on any exception — which `_plan_review_precheck` converts to a blocking `CommandError`. Gate-disabled (`off`) skips the check entirely (transition.py:661–662). `--force` remains the escape and is audit-logged.

---

**Summary:** All seven acceptance criteria are demonstrably satisfied by the implementation in `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py`, `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py`, `src/rebar/_commands/txn.py`, `src/rebar/signing.py`, and `src/rebar/config.py`, with interface-level test coverage in `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py` and unit-level coverage in `tests/unit/test_plan_review.py`.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->
## Why
Claim (open->in_progress) is the enforcement point and must be fast (no LLM, no network round-trip).
## What
- Claim requires a valid, non-stale signed attestation; a FAST signature-verify only (target p95 < ~50ms);
absence/stale signature blocks; --force + justification override (recorded).
## Scope
- The heavy review runs OUT-OF-BAND (rebar review-plan / an MCP run_workflow), triggered on a claim-block or by CI.
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Claim blocks on absent/material-edit-invalidated signature; the fast check meets the latency target.
- [ ] --force override is available and logged with justification.
- [ ] No LLM/network on the claim path.
## Acceptance Criteria (added — close G3 coverage)

- [ ] End-to-end proving check: claim-block (absent/stale signature) → run review-plan → earn a signature → claim succeeds, PLUS edge cases (--force + logging; material-edit invalidation; fail-open on unsupported stack; cap-hit INDETERMINATE; gate-off preference leaves claim at today's behavior).

- [ ] ENFORCE the config preference (defaults OFF): off → claim/transition-to-in_progress unchanged; on → the fast signature check gates them. (The config TOOL/UX is the separate F5 epic; the claim path ENFORCES the preference.)
## Acceptance Criteria (added — T9 claim atomicity)

- [ ] The signature-verify + the open→in_progress transition are ONE atomic operation (reuse rebar's atomic `claim`: re-read under lock + optimistic concurrency), with no check-then-act gap — concurrently, exactly one claim wins; cross-clone double-claims converge per rebar's replay semantics.
## Acceptance Criteria (added — signing-infra unavailability)

- [ ] When the gate is ENABLED and the signing/verification machinery is UNAVAILABLE (missing MCP environment key / signing subsystem error), claim FAILS CLOSED (blocks — consistent with the completion-verifier close gate's fail-closed posture), with the recorded `--force`+justification as the escape. When the gate is DISABLED (default OFF), claim is unchanged regardless. (A missing signing KEY is an INTEGRITY dependency — distinct from review-TIME polyglot tool fail-open, which is about coverage.)

content.text:
IMPLEMENTATION NOTES (validated in sandbox, 2026-06-09) — SOURCE CORRECTION, READ FIRST:
TWO code paths need the guard. The dispatcher routes `rebar edit` through ticket_edit in ticket-lib-api.sh (~:1514 field-guard case), NOT through ticket-edit.sh — patching only ticket-edit.sh does NOT fix the CLI. Validated: add a title=* arm to BOTH field-guard cases (ticket-lib-api.sh ~:1514 with `return 1`, ticket-edit.sh ~:120 with `exit 1`):
   title=*)
       if [ -z "${_pair#title=}" ]; then
           echo "Error: --title requires a non-empty value (empty values silently clobber the title; bug 4f50)" >&2
           return 1   # exit 1 in ticket-edit.sh
       fi
       ;;
Validated: edit --title="" exit 1, title unchanged, on BOTH the CLI path and direct ticket-edit.sh invocation; --title="Renamed" still works.
Regression suites: test-ticket-edit.sh, test-ticket-lib-api.sh, test-ticket-cli-baseline-fixture.sh, test-ticket-id-resolution-{hardening,direct-exec}.sh, test-ticket-platform-compat.sh.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

CORRECTION to ## Source: primary fix site is ticket-lib-api.sh ticket_edit field-guard case (~:1514) — the dispatcher routes rebar edit there, NOT through ticket-edit.sh. Patch BOTH (see implementation notes above).

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->
## Summary
edit --title="" silently clobbers the title; the identical clobber class was already fixed for --description (bug e78f-9f79) and --parent, but not --title.
## Reproduction Steps
(verified on installed 0.4.0 == HEAD, temp store)
1. T=$(rebar create task "real title" | tail -1)
2. rebar edit $T --title="" -> exit 0
3. rebar show $T | jq .title -> ""
4. Contrast: rebar create task "" -> Error: title must be non-empty (exit 1); rebar edit $T --description="" -> Error (citing e78f-9f79).
## Expected vs Actual
- Expected: empty --title rejected like empty --description (and like create's title validation).
- Actual: empty title accepted, producing "" titles that break list/search ergonomics.
## Source
src/rebar/_engine/ticket-edit.sh — description guard :122-127 (the model), parent guard :156; no equivalent in the --title arm.
## Fix direction
Mirror the e78f-9f79 guard for --title (validated diff in the implementation-notes comment).
## Suggested RED test
edit --title="" -> exit 1, title unchanged; edit --title="new" still works.
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Empty --title rejected with a clear error naming the rule (RED test first)
- [ ] Non-empty title edits unchanged
- [ ] Sweep ticket-edit.sh for any other empty-value clobber arms (tags? assignee has legit clear semantics — document which empties are intentional)

content.text:
Brainstormed + decomposed (see session_log rich-keel-pen / 31d6). Focus: CODE drift only (problem-2 ticket-field invalidation already shipped in dead-stalk-chasm). Model: dependency-scope, NO attribution (sibling exemption dissolves; content-addressed per-file hash signed into the manifest). Children: Story 1 jerky-vista-amok (22bf) = drift detect+invalidate (full re-review) + new DET P9 empty-file-impact warning; fixes worm-folly-barge over-invalidation. Story 2 grace-thud-feint (5a6c, depends_on S1) = narrowed delta-re-review over the enforced CODEBASE_GROUNDED set + chained attestation; descoped read-set capture & >N-files fallback per brainstorm.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

PASS 1 review — criterion T10 (Infrastructure / IaC) [overlay]: NOT APPLICABLE / PASS. No findings.

This plan concerns code-drift invalidation for plan-review attestations — application/review-pipeline logic: detecting material drift in the code a signed review was grounded against (the resolved edit-set / cited files) and invalidating-or-flagging the attestation, cheaply, off the hot path. It provisions or configures no infrastructure: no cloud resources, no IaC (Terraform/CloudFormation/CDK/Pulumi/Ansible), and no Kubernetes/Helm. The "code it was grounded against" is source files resolved via the existing code-grounding oracle, not deployed infrastructure.

None of T10's binary checks engage: no remote-state/locking or plan-before-apply discipline, no IAM roles/policies or long-lived credentials, no idempotency/out-of-band-drift apply surface, no blast-radius / destroy-replace / prevent_destroy on stateful resources, no plaintext-secrets-in-IaC concern, no expensive/unbounded-resource sizing, and no new-infra logging/metrics/alarms or teardown. (Note: the AC's "drift" is CODE drift relative to a review, not Terraform/IaC configuration drift — so the T10(c) idempotency/drift check does not apply.) The signing/attestation machinery this builds on (parent epic 5fd2) is an HMAC-over-event-log scheme, not infra.

Per the T10 overlay gate ("apply only when the plan provisions or configures infrastructure ... else PASS not-applicable") and its ANTI-FP rule (not-applicable for non-infra tickets), T10 PASSES as not-applicable.

Affirmations: T10 — not-applicable (no infrastructure provisioning or configuration in scope).

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

PASS 1 review — criterion T11 (Data-migration / backfill safety) [overlay]: PASS, not-applicable.

This plan ("Code-drift invalidation for plan-review attestations") adds a code-drift detection mechanism that invalidates or flags a plan-review attestation when the code it was grounded against (the resolved edit-set / cited files) drifts after the review. It introduces no schema change to an existing persisted format and performs no data backfill:

- The drift signal is an ADDITIVE record over the parent epic's existing append-only attestation scheme (SIGNATURE head-state event + reducer-ignored REVIEW_RESULT sidecar). Going-forward attestations would carry a new grounding fingerprint; pre-feature attestations simply lack it and are treated as legacy/ungrounded — there is no in-place rewrite of existing rows and no backfill obligation stated or implied.
- No blocking DDL on a large table, no giant backfill transaction, no dual-write/cutover window, and no partial-failure data-loss surface exists for T11's binary checks (online/expand-contract, batching/throttling, resumability/idempotency, dual-write window, rollback/data-loss) to evaluate.

Per the T11 overlay gate ("apply only when the plan changes a schema/persisted format or backfills data; else PASS not-applicable") and its ANTI-FP rule (not-applicable for non-persisted/in-memory changes), this is a clean not-applicable. Consistent with the parent epic's own measured prior that rebar-domain "migration" tickets are routinely T11-not-applicable (recorded dry run: deterministic T11 fired 2/19 plans, all false — the bash→Python strangler, not a data migration).

Affirmations: [T11] not-applicable — no schema/persisted-format change and no data backfill in scope; additive-only record over an existing append-only event scheme.

No T11 findings.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

PASS-1 review — criterion T1 (prior-art / novel-architecture justification, overlay).

APPLICABILITY: a T1 bright-line fires. The epic proposes a NOVEL architectural pattern — content-/fingerprint-addressed freshness invalidation layered onto a cryptographic (HMAC) attestation — and carries a performance target ("cheap enough not to tax hot paths"). So the overlay applies and the plan should consider prior art before committing.

AFFIRMATIONS (T1 checks the epic TEXT satisfies):
- (c) No unverified capability assertions. The epic asserts no "library supports X" claim; the mechanism is described abstractly (detect material drift in the grounded files) and grounds itself by spinning out of a real, shipped predecessor (5fd2), whose attestation machinery exists in-repo (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py). Nothing unresolved to fail-closed on.
- Scoping is honest: the AC is decomposed into detect → invalidate/flag → tested, and explicitly bounds itself to "the code it was grounded against" rather than over-claiming whole-repo semantics.

FINDINGS:

- [ ] State, in the EPIC body, the prior art the drift mechanism is modeled on (content-addressed / Merkle input-hashing; Gerrit copyCondition `has:unchanged-files`; Bazel input-hashing; proof-caching dependency checksums) AND the existing in-repo binding it refines, so the novel-pattern choice is justified at the epic altitude rather than only in a child.
  - criteria: T1
  - location: epic description "Acceptance Criteria" line 1 ("A mechanism detects material drift in the code the review was grounded against (e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files).") + "Context"
  - evidence:
    - The epic text considers NO prior art and leaves the mechanism as a bare "e.g." — it does not name content-addressing, Merkle hashing, Bazel/Gerrit incremental-staleness precedents, or any established approach, despite proposing exactly that class of mechanism. ABSENCE rationale: for an overlay-triggering novel pattern, T1(a)/(b) want the prior-art consideration visible in the plan being committed to.
    - The relevant prior art DOES exist and the team HAS done the analysis — but it lives in the child story `22bf` ("This matches the consensus of every sound incremental-staleness system (Gerrit copyCondition has:unchanged-files, Bazel input-hashing, proof-caching dependency checksums)"), not in this epic. The epic-level plan a reviewer/implementer reads first therefore reads as reinventing a staleness check with no prior-art grounding.
    - Load-bearing IN-REPO prior art the epic omits: the attestation ALREADY has a freshness binding — `attest.py::claim_gate_check` invalidates on `result.head_sha != head` (coarse, whole-HEAD). The epic should frame its mechanism as REFINING this existing `head_sha` binding (whole-commit → grounded-file-scoped), not as a net-new capability; without that framing the relationship to the shipped mechanism (and the over-invalidation it causes) is invisible at epic level.
  - suggested_fix: Add a short "Prior art / relationship to existing binding" note to the epic: (1) name the existing coarse `head_sha` staleness binding in `attest.py` this refines; (2) name the established content-addressed/input-hashing precedents (Bazel input-hashing, Gerrit `copyCondition has:unchanged-files`, Merkle dependency checksums) the design adopts; (3) one line on why content-addressing (ancestry/rebase-independent) is chosen over the whole-commit binding. The child `22bf` already contains this material — lift the summary up to the epic.

This is the only T1 finding; the chunk is otherwise clean for this pass.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

PASS 1 review — criterion T11 (Data-migration / backfill safety) [overlay]: PASS / not-applicable. No findings.

T11 is an overlay that applies ONLY when the plan changes a schema / persisted format or backfills data. This plan does neither as written: it specifies a MECHANISM to detect material code drift in the code a review was grounded against (the resolved edit-set / cited files) and to invalidate or flag the attestation as stale when that code drifts. The three Acceptance Criteria are all mechanism/behaviour + test, not a persisted-format change or a data backfill:
- AC1 "A mechanism detects material drift in the code the review was grounded against (e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files)" — detection logic, no schema migration.
- AC2 "Drift invalidates or flags the attestation, prompting re-review; cheap enough not to tax hot paths" — invalidation/flag behaviour, no backfill.
- AC3 "Tested: code drift after a signed review -> attestation no longer valid" — a test, no migration-execution surface.

None of T11's binary checks engage: there is no online/expand-contract DDL, no large batched/throttled backfill, no resumable partial-migration, no dual-write window, and no partial-failure data-loss/rollback surface to evaluate. Per the overlay's ANTI-FP rule (not-applicable for non-persisted/in-memory changes), T11 PASSES as not-applicable.

Affirmations: [T11] not-applicable — the plan changes no schema/persisted format and backfills no data; it adds drift-detection + invalidation behaviour over the existing attestation, so the migration-EXECUTION safety overlay does not apply.

Disambiguation note (NOT a T11 finding): IF the implementation chooses to persist NEW grounding metadata on the attestation (e.g. the cited-file set or a code fingerprint) and existing signed attestations would need that field, the additive/coexistence concern for that new persisted data is the province of T12 (rollout/rollback) and the sibling parent epic's preserved-and-ignored event-rollout convention — not T11 migration-execution. The plan text does not affirmatively state such a persisted-format change, so it remains not-applicable here.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

PASS-1 PLAN REVIEW — [G6] Approach soundness, anti-patterns & alternative-selection. One MINOR finding (missing positive rationale in the epic body) + affirmations. Grounded the mechanism reasoning in the live attestation code.

AFFIRMATIONS (G6 sub-checks this plan satisfies):

- MECHANISM CORRECTNESS: The chosen mechanism — content-addressed (Merkle) per-file binding of the review's dependency set, NO per-commit attribution — actually achieves the goal and is sound against the edge cases G6 probes. Verified against source: today's freshness is a whole-HEAD exact match (`attest.py` `claim_gate_check` step 3, lines 96-102: `result.get("head_sha") != head` → `stale-head`) and `head_sha` is only audit metadata, NOT in the signed payload (`build_manifest` lines 33-41 omit it) — so the plan's premise (too-coarse in both directions; latent integrity gap) is accurate. The hard edge cases are handled: empty dependency set → conservative invalidate-on-any-commit fallback (child 22bf AC3); deletion / unexpected-creation → drift (22bf AC2); content-addressing makes it ancestry-independent (rebase/shared-checkout HEAD-move immune). No hidden ordering/atomicity/TOCTOU assumption at the epic level.

- FITNESS-FOR-PURPOSE: Solves the NAMED problem (code drift silently staling the attestation), not a proxy — it scopes freshness to "what the review depended on" rather than re-using the coarse whole-commit signal. The narrowed delta-re-review (child 5a6c) correctly narrows along the harness-ENFORCED `CODEBASE_GROUNDED = {E4,G1G2,A1,G6}` partition (verified the enforcement is a real, cited invariant), not along fragile per-commit attribution.

- APPROACH SELECTION (reviewer-generated alternatives): I generated two structurally-different alternatives and judged the chosen approach defensible. (a) Per-commit attribution / sibling-exemption (control-flow alternative) — the plan explicitly dissolves this (content-addressed hashing makes a sibling's edit to a shared file identical to any edit); defensibly simpler + more robust. (b) Observed read-set capture (data-source alternative) — explicitly considered and rejected in child 5a6c with a sound reason (a read does not imply load-bearing; casts too wide a net). Both are defensibly NOT superior → discarded, not written into the children (correct anti-priming). No clearly-superior missed alternative.

FINDING (G6.4 — positive rationale absent from the EPIC body):

- [ ] State the POSITIVE rationale for the chosen content-addressed-dependency-scope approach in the epic's own description, not only in the decomposition comment. The epic body's `## Context` states the PROBLEM (code can drift after review) and the success/acceptance criteria, but gives no "why content-addressed dependency-set scoping fits" rationale — that reasoning (dependency-scope, NO attribution, matches Gerrit `has:unchanged-files` / Bazel input-hashing / proof-caching checksums) lives ONLY in the post-decomposition comment and the children (22bf `## Why`). G6 requires the plan to state a positive rationale for the chosen approach; an epic whose description names the goal but not the chosen mechanism's fit leaves the next reader to reconstruct it from a comment.
  - criteria: G6
  - location: epic `## Context` / `## Success Criteria` (description body) — no rationale for the chosen approach; cf. the decomposition comment ("Model: dependency-scope, NO attribution; content-addressed per-file hash signed into the manifest") and child 22bf `## Why`.
  - evidence: the description body states only "the underlying CODE the plan reasons about can drift AFTER the review ... silently staling the attestation"; the chosen-approach rationale appears only in the 2026 decomposition comment and the children, not in the epic plan text reviewers/implementers read first.
  - suggested_fix: Add a one/two-line "Approach" note to the description, e.g. "Bind freshness to the CONTENT of the review's dependency files (file_impact ∪ cited files) via a signed content-hash map, NOT to the whole commit — matching the consensus of sound incremental-staleness systems (Gerrit has:unchanged-files, Bazel input-hashing); no per-commit attribution, so the sibling-exemption concern dissolves." This states the positive fit without listing rejected alternatives in the implementer-facing body.

Net: the approach is sound, fit-for-purpose, and defensibly best-available; the only G6 gap is that the epic's own body under-states the positive rationale that its comment + children already carry well.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

PASS-1 review — criterion T3 (Integration feasibility) [overlay]: PASS / not-applicable. No findings.

T3 applies only when a plan integrates an EXTERNAL API/CLI/service/library or asserts a capability it has not used before; otherwise it is not-applicable. This plan integrates none. Every mechanism it depends on is an INTERNAL, already-used rebar subsystem that the parent epic 5fd2 already consumes:

- Code-drift detection over "the resolved edit-set / cited files" rides on the shipped, polyglot code-grounding oracle (src/rebar/grounding/ — refute_absence/applies/scan/resolve, exported from grounding/__init__.py), the same facade Layer-1 already calls. The brainstorm's "content-addressed per-file hash signed into the manifest" is a stdlib file-hash, not an external dependency.
- Invalidate/flag-the-attestation reuses the existing HMAC-SHA256 signing + SIGNATURE-event + manifest scheme (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py, src/rebar/signing.py) — the manifest already binds a `material:` fingerprint and a git `head_sha`, so adding a grounding/code fingerprint is an additive line on a proven manifest, not a new integration. No new key custody.

Per the overlay's ANTI-FP rule ("an internal, already-used integration is not-applicable"), T3 PASSES. No external subcommand/endpoint to verify, no auth/HTTPS precondition, no asserted-but-unverified external capability, and no capability gap that would route to a SPIKE.

Affirmations (T3): integration-as-described is achievable via existing internals (no capability gap); named mechanisms (grounding oracle facade; the signing/manifest scheme) resolve to real, already-consumed in-repo source — MATCH; auth/HTTPS not applicable (no external service); no SPIKE needed.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

PASS-1 review — criterion T8 (LLM/prompt structural-completeness probe) [overlay]: PASS — no findings.

Applicability: T8 is an overlay that fires only when the plan DEFINES an LLM/agent system (prompts, sub-agents, reviewers, output schemas, enums). This plan does not: it is a deterministic drift-detection mechanism that compares the code an attestation was grounded against (the resolved edit-set / cited files) to the current code state and invalidates-or-flags the attestation. It authors no prompt, sub-agent, reviewer, output schema, or enum vocabulary of its own. It is a CONSUMER of the attestation/coverage record produced out-of-band by the parent plan-review gate (5fd2). So the overlay's structural-gap probes are largely not-applicable; the few enum-like terms it references resolve to already-specified vocabulary elsewhere.

Affirmations (T8 checklist items this chunk satisfies):
- No schema/enum referenced whose value vocabulary is never defined. The drift mechanism reasons over the attestation's existing artifacts — the coverage record / cited files / resolved edit-set + the signature's head_sha binding — all of which are already-defined contracts from the parent gate (the SIGNATURE event's head_sha freshness binding + recorded coverage). Method note: grounded against attest.py:96-124, signing.py:19-25/319-360, and tickets 5fd2/22bf/a36b/5a6c. Findings 8/9 cite accurate code; 10/11 rest on an inference contradicted by the live ticket graph.

Both findings share one root error: treating a child ticket's title/existence as an externally-expressed requirement the plan silently contradicts or omits, when the children ARE the plan's own decomposition that resolves these very points (22bf+5a6c are the two implementing stories; a36b is a sibling bug whose fix the plan adopts). [12] a36b ("worm-folly-barge") is a BUG whose title/description name "ANY new commit (even unrelated) invalidates a fresh attestation" as the DEFECT TO FIX, not a requirement the plan must honor. a36b AC1: "Claim freshness is scoped to the code the review was grounded against (resolved edit-set / cited files) rather than the whole HEAD sha, so an unrelated commit does NOT stale the attestation." Expected: "claim succeeds … none of the code … changed." That is the SAME material-only scoping the plan adopts — the plan AGREES with a36b, it does not narrow away from it. ANY-commit-invalidates survives only as the empty-set conservative FALLBACK in sibling 22bf (unscopable tickets), not a broad dropped requirement. Citation inverted. Duplicates prior PASS-2 finding [10] on this ticket.

Decomposition comment: "Story 2 grace-thud-feint (5a6c, depends_on S1) = narrowed delta-re-review." cited_reference_accurate: [12]=no (a36b says the inverse of what the finding asserts); [13]=na (5a6c correctly named, but the dropped-inference is wrong — it is an open child of this epic).

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->
## Context
A plan-review attestation must stay valid exactly as long as the thing it certifies — the plan,
reviewed against the code it reasons about — is unchanged. Two facets of the current binding are
wrong, and this epic fixes both (decomposition + full rationale: session log `rich-keel-pen`,
ADR `docs/adr/0002-code-drift-invalidation.md`):
- **Over-invalidation (the LIVE bug — `worm-folly-barge`/a36b).** `attest.py::claim_gate_check`
binds freshness to the exact code HEAD sha, so ANY commit — even an unrelated file or a docs
typo — invalidates a freshly-earned, still-correct attestation, forcing a slow, billable
re-review for no semantic reason. (So code drift is NOT "silent" today — the binding is simply
too COARSE; the original framing of this epic as a pure under-invalidation problem was wrong and
is corrected here.)
- **Under-invalidation (precision goal).** Because the binding is whole-HEAD rather than scoped to
the code the review depended on, once we narrow it we must NOT lose the ability to detect that the
relevant code actually changed.
Both share one root cause: the code-binding is whole-HEAD instead of scoped to the review's
dependency set. The material-fingerprint binding (problem-2, already shipped in `dead-stalk-chasm`:
invalidate on description/AC/file_impact/decomposition edits, not on tags/comments/links/assignee)
is correct and stays; this epic adds a SEPARATE, independent code-drift check ANDed with it.
## Chosen approach (and why)
**Content-addressed, dependency-scoped, no attribution.** Invalidate on *what the review depended
on*, not *who/what changed the rest of the tree*. This is the consensus of every sound
incremental-staleness system (Gerrit `copyCondition` `has:unchanged-files`, Bazel input-hashing,
proof-caching dependency checksums) — none decide staleness by attributing a change to an author, so
the "sibling exemption" concern dissolves: a sibling's edit to a shared file is treated like any
edit. Concretely:
- **Dependency set** = the ticket's declared `file_impact` ∪ the files cited in its `REVIEW_RESULT`
findings (whole-file granularity in v1; region/AST narrowing is a possible future, not configured
now).
- **Binding** = a per-path `{path: content-hash}` map stored INSIDE the signed manifest (so drift
detection is itself tamper-evident; today `head_sha` is unsigned audit metadata). Content-
addressing is ancestry-independent — immune to rebases and shared-checkout HEAD moves.
- **Decision = INVALIDATE (hard block), not flag-stale.** A drifted dependency blocks the claim and
prompts re-review, consistent with the existing gate (absent/stale signature already blocks). We do
NOT ship a soft warning for drift.
- **Empty dependency set** (no `file_impact`, no citations) → conservative fallback: invalidate on
any commit (today's safe behavior, now only for unscopable tickets). A new DET-floor check (`P9`)
emits an advisory nudge to populate `file_impact` so scoping works.
- **Re-review may be narrowed (Story 2).** On pure code drift, re-running only the enforced
code-grounded criteria (`CODEBASE_GROUNDED`) is sound and cheaper than a full re-review; the
full re-review is the fallback.
## Hot-path budget
The drift check runs on the **claim path** (the fast, no-LLM, no-network HMAC verify; target p95
< ~50ms). Hashing the handful of dependency files is sub-millisecond, so it stays well within that
budget; the check must add no LLM/network call to claim.
## Rollout / back-out
The check lives behind the existing `verify.require_plan_review_for_claim` gate. Attestations signed
*before* this lands carry no `{path: hash}` map in their manifest; on first claim under the new code
they are treated as needing re-review (re-run `review-plan` to earn a map-bearing attestation) —
acceptable because re-review is the normal recovery. Back-out = revert the drift check; the
material-fingerprint binding is untouched, so reverting cannot corrupt existing attestations.
## Decomposition
- **Story 1 — `jerky-vista-amok` (22bf):** drift detection + invalidation (full re-review on drift)
+ the `P9` empty-`file_impact` DET warning. Fixes `worm-folly-barge`.
- **Story 2 — `grace-thud-feint` (5a6c, depends_on Story 1):** narrowed delta-re-review over the
enforced code-grounded set + chained attestation; measured before default-on.
- **Bug — `worm-folly-barge` (a36b):** the over-invalidation failing-case report; its remedy is
owned and implemented by Story 1 and it closes when Story 1 lands.
## Success Criteria
- An attestation is invalidated when the code it was grounded against (its dependency set) drifts
materially, AND is NOT invalidated by changes outside that set.
- The drift check is content-addressed and cheap enough to run on the claim hot path.
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A mechanism detects material drift in the review's dependency set (`file_impact` ∪ cited
files), via a per-path content-hash map signed into the attestation manifest.
- [ ] Drift **invalidates** the attestation (hard block, prompting re-review); an unrelated change
does NOT invalidate (fixes `worm-folly-barge`). Empty dependency set → conservative fallback.
- [ ] The check runs on the claim path within its latency budget (no LLM/network; sub-ms hashing).
- [ ] The novel decision is documented (ADR `0002-code-drift-invalidation.md`) and the manifest
payload shape is pinned so Story 2's per-finding code-hash vintage composes on it.
- [ ] Tested: code drift in a dependency file after a signed review → attestation invalid; an
unrelated commit → attestation stays valid.

content.text:
COMPLETED (commit 24fc4901).

Repackaged the in-process engine Python as real rebar.* subpackages and removed the library-path sys.path insertion:
- ticket_reducer -> rebar.reducer; ticket_graph -> rebar.graph; ticket_resolver/ticket_output/ticket_reads -> rebar._engine_support.{resolver,output,reads}. Intra-package imports made relative; graph reaches the reducer via a direct rebar.reducer import (one fewer importlib-by-path load).
- _native.py/_reads.py import the subpackages directly; engine dir is no longer on the library sys.path.
- Thin compat shims kept in rebar/_engine/ under the old top-level names for the bash dispatcher + python3 helpers; each self-bootstraps the rebar package from its own __file__ (so a bare engine python3 needs no PYTHONPATH help), aliases the real module via sys.modules, mirrors submodules for identity, and forwards __main__ when bash runs it as a script. Retire with the strangler-fig ports (adult-oxide-slave).

AC status:
- AC1 (no generic top-level name importable from the library path): MET — hermetic-subprocess regression test in tests/unit/test_engine_dir.py.
- AC2 (wheel has no __pycache__/.pyc): MET — explicit pyproject exclude + in-process hatchling build test; hatchling added to dev deps.
- AC3 (full suite green; sys.modules identity-keys decision recorded): MET — 1475 passed on the non-integration suite (the lone red is the pre-existing load-sensitive async-push timing flake test-rebar-push-policy.sh, which passes 3/3 standalone and touches no moved module). Decision recorded in docs/architecture.md.

Scope decisions (per the 'relocate in-process pkgs only' call):
- rebar_reconciler LEFT in the engine dir: never imported in-process (subprocess via 'python -m rebar_reconciler' + mode.py by-path in mcp_server), so it leaks no generic name onto the library path. Its sys.modules identity-key cleanup (the spec_from_file_location dotted-key scheme) is left to tangly-abbey-smelt, which this repackage unblocks.
- ticket_txn.py LEFT in the engine dir: subprocess-only write core, invoked by absolute path, never on the library path.

Breaking-window item: shipped inside the window (import-path rename done while there are no external consumers).

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->
## Why

Rec 5 of session-logs/2026-06-09-architecture-review.md (risk R7). src/rebar/_native.py inserts the engine dir into sys.path so ticket_reducer, ticket_graph, ticket_resolver, rebar_reconciler import as TOP-LEVEL module names; engine_env() does the same via PYTHONPATH for subprocesses. Generic top-level names risk host collisions, defeat static tooling, and extend the unpacked-install constraint beyond bash. BREAKING-WINDOW: an import-path rename is free today (zero external users); in six months it is a deprecation program.
## What

- ticket_reducer -> rebar.reducer; ticket_graph -> rebar.graph; rebar_reconciler -> rebar.reconciler; ticket_txn/ticket_output/ticket_resolver -> rebar._engine_support (or fold into a future rebar._commands).
- Delete the sys.path insertion in _native.py; restrict engine_env()'s PYTHONPATH hack to bash-only callers.
- Keep thin top-level shim modules re-exporting the new locations ONLY while bash heredocs still import old names; delete them with the strangler-fig Tier A/B ports.
- Exclude __pycache__ from the wheel; verify the pyproject artifacts glob output (src/rebar/_engine/__pycache__/*.pyc currently at risk of shipping).
## Scope
