content.text:
G4 child-consistency review (parent 3fe1 + this reconciler-sweep child, whole sibling roster considered). Two high-confidence cross-child findings; the broad-except / characterization-test / rollback scope is otherwise clean and consistent with the parent.

---
FINDING 1 — Dependency gap + conflicting assumptions: migrated reconciler diagnostics log to a HANDLER-LESS logger in a NAMESPACE the substrate never wires up.

criteria: [parent SC2 "failures that used to vanish silently are now observable on a stderr logger"; parent AC1 logging substrate; this child AC: "Migrate stderr-diagnostic prints (reconcile.py, __main__.py, etc.) to the named logger"]
location: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/__main__.py (main, ~L266), src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py; vs sibling 8fbd (logging substrate) file_impact {src/rebar/__init__.py, cli.py, _cli/__init__.py, mcp_server.py}
evidence:
  - The reconciler is a SEPARATE SUBPROCESS entrypoint: `python -m rebar_reconciler` with its own main() in __main__.py:266, launched by the GHA reconcile-bridge workflow / inject-and-heal.sh / e2e probes (scripts/jira-pressure-test/*.sh:75,135 `python -m rebar_reconciler "$@" 2>&1`). README.md:215-225 + src/rebar/_engine.py:75-96 confirm it ships as on-disk package DATA and runs out-of-process.
  - Sibling 8fbd installs a stderr StreamHandler ONLY at the CLI main (cli.py / _cli/__init__.py) and the MCP main (mcp_server.py:main), and the NullHandler on the "rebar" ROOT logger in src/rebar/__init__.py. It explicitly does NOT touch the reconciler's __main__.main(). Confirmed: no StreamHandler/basicConfig exists anywhere under src/rebar today (grep: 0 hits) — matches the audit's "no handler configured anywhere".
  - The reconciler's modules import as the TOP-LEVEL `rebar_reconciler` package (from rebar_reconciler import acli, etc.; _engine.py:82-85 prepends the engine dir to PYTHONPATH for this), so the convention's `logging.getLogger(__name__)` resolves to `rebar_reconciler.<module>` — NOT under the `rebar` root logger where 8fbd attaches the NullHandler/handler. The reconciler already does this in ~16 modules (apply_outbound.py:27, batch_dispatch.py:27, acli_subprocess.py:23, ...).
  - rebar_reconciler/__init__.py is empty (one comment line) — no NullHandler, no handler. __main__.main() installs no handler.
scenarios:
  - After this child migrates a `print(..., file=sys.stderr)` INFO/diagnostic in reconcile.py/__main__.py to `logger.info(...)`/`logger.warning(...)`, the reconciler subprocess has NO handler on the `rebar_reconciler` logger tree → INFO is dropped entirely; WARNING+ only reaches Python's last-resort stderr in a different format. A diagnostic that USED to be visible (and captured by the probes' `2>&1` side-car) now vanishes — the exact inversion of parent SC2.
impact: This pairing cannot meet its own AC ("migrate prints to the named logger") AND parent SC2 ("observable on a stderr logger") simultaneously, because no sibling stands up a handler for the reconciler subprocess / the `rebar_reconciler` logger namespace. The substrate child's two-entrypoint design (CLI + MCP) silently omits rebar's THIRD entrypoint. Absence cross-checked against the whole roster: no sibling (8fbd, 16a1, 8d01, d360, ceaa, fd72, 0ec1) installs a handler at __main__.main() or bridges rebar_reconciler.* to the rebar root logger.
checklist_item: dependency gap; conflicting assumptions
suggested_fix: Either (a) add to this child's scope (or re-scope 8fbd) a stderr StreamHandler install at rebar_reconciler/__main__.py:main() — the reconciler's own entrypoint — and/or (b) explicitly bridge the `rebar_reconciler` logger to the `rebar` root (propagation won't help across distinct root names) so migrated diagnostics actually reach stderr. Add an acceptance bullet here asserting reconciler-subprocess diagnostics land on stderr, and add a cross-dep on 8fbd. If the decision is to NOT migrate reconciler prints (keep file=sys.stderr) given it is a distinct entrypoint, strike the "migrate stderr-diagnostic prints ... to the named logger" bullet to remove the contradiction.

---
FINDING 2 — Consumer impact: the `ERROR:`-prefix stderr contract that operators/probes grep for is broken by the print->logger migration.

criteria: [parent SC2 stdout/stderr observability; this child AC: "keep any acli/jira data output semantics intact"]
location: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/__main__.py:401-417 (and the `ERROR:`-prefixed stderr prints), e2e probes scripts/jira-pressure-test/e2e_validation_probe.sh / e2e_field_validation_probe.sh
evidence:
  - __main__.py:401-404 documents that diagnostics are emitted to stderr specifically so "probes that look for the `ERROR:` prefix" and "the probe's unfiltered side-car log" capture them; __main__.py:413-416 prints `ERROR: --filter-local-ids ...` to stderr.
  - The probes capture reconciler output via `python -m rebar_reconciler "$@" 2>&1` (e2e_validation_probe.sh:75, e2e_field_validation_probe.sh:135) and grep that combined stream.
scenarios:
  - Migrating these `print("ERROR: ...", file=sys.stderr)` lines to `logger.error(...)` changes the line prefix/format (logger default has no `ERROR:` token unless a formatter is configured — and per Finding 1 no handler/formatter is configured for this subprocess at all), so a probe greppping for `ERROR:` stops matching.
impact: A consumer (the jira-pressure-test probes) silently loses its failure signal — a residual-reference/consumer-impact mode unguarded by any sibling. This is exactly the kind of "best-effort migration that breaks a machine contract" the parent's stdout-is-sacred section guards for, but on the stderr/probe side.
checklist_item: consumer impact; residual references
suggested_fix: When migrating reconciler diagnostics, either preserve the `ERROR:` token via a configured formatter on the reconciler handler (depends on Finding 1's handler existing), or explicitly carve out the probe-contract `ERROR:`-prefixed stderr prints as data-like output that stays as `print(..., file=sys.stderr)` (parallel to the CLI "stdout data prints stay" carve-out). Add an AC bullet asserting the probe `ERROR:`-grep contract is preserved.

---
Modes checked and clean for this pairing: implicit shared state (the _errors.py helpers are read-only/shared-by-design, characterization-tested first — fine); scope overlap (reconciler package is uniquely owned by this child; no other sweep touches _engine/rebar_reconciler/); ordering violation (LATE/last sequencing + blocks 0ec1 + downstream of 16a1 is consistent). The two findings above are the dependency-gap/conflicting-assumptions and consumer-impact modes.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

G4 re-confirmation (parent 3fe1 + this reconciler-sweep child, whole roster considered). Independently re-verified the two prior cross-child findings against source; both hold high-confidence.

Verified facts:
- `rebar_reconciler` is a TOP-LEVEL package put on PYTHONPATH for subprocess use only (_engine.py:75-96, `python -m rebar_reconciler`), so its ~16 `logging.getLogger(__name__)` loggers resolve under `rebar_reconciler.*`, NOT the `rebar` root.
- `rebar_reconciler/__init__.py` is a single comment line — no NullHandler.
- No `StreamHandler` exists anywhere under `src/rebar` today (0 hits) — matches the audit's "no handler configured anywhere"; substrate child 8fbd only installs handlers at CLI + MCP mains and a NullHandler on the `rebar` ROOT, leaving the reconciler subprocess + `rebar_reconciler.*` namespace unwired.
- Probes capture `python -m rebar_reconciler "$@" 2>&1` (e2e_validation_probe.sh:75, e2e_field_validation_probe.sh:135) and grep `^(FILTERED|filter:|OK:|ERROR:)` (17 sites), consuming the `ERROR:`-prefixed stderr prints (__main__.py:413-416, documented at :398-404).

FINDING 1 — Dependency gap + conflicting assumptions: this child's AC "migrate stderr-diagnostic prints (reconcile.py, __main__.py) to the named logger" cannot coexist with parent SC2 "observable on a stderr logger", because no sibling stands up a handler for the reconciler's third subprocess entrypoint or bridges `rebar_reconciler.*` to the `rebar` root. Migrated INFO is dropped; WARNING+ falls to Python's last-resort handler in a different format. Cross-checked whole roster: no sibling installs a handler at `rebar_reconciler/__main__.main()`.
  suggested_fix: re-scope 8fbd (or this child) to install a stderr StreamHandler at `rebar_reconciler/__main__.py:main()` and/or bridge `rebar_reconciler` to `rebar`; add an AC asserting reconciler-subprocess diagnostics land on stderr; add cross-dep on 8fbd. Else strike the print->logger migration bullet to remove the contradiction.

FINDING 2 — Consumer impact / residual references: migrating the `ERROR:`-prefixed stderr prints to `logger.error(...)` breaks the `^ERROR:` grep contract the jira-pressure-test probes depend on (no formatter emits an `ERROR:` token, and per Finding 1 no handler/formatter is configured for this subprocess). A consumer silently loses its failure signal — the stderr-side analogue of the parent's stdout-is-sacred constraint.
  suggested_fix: preserve the `ERROR:` token via a configured formatter on the reconciler handler, OR carve out the probe-contract `ERROR:`-prefixed stderr prints as data-like output that stays `print(..., file=sys.stderr)` (parallel to the CLI stdout-data carve-out). Add an AC asserting the probe `ERROR:`-grep contract is preserved.

Modes clean for this pairing: implicit shared state (_errors.py helpers shared read-only, characterization-tested first); scope overlap (reconciler package uniquely owned — no other sweep touches _engine/rebar_reconciler/); ordering violation (LATE/last + blocks 0ec1 + downstream of 16a1 is consistent).

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

PASS 1 review — criterion E4 (assumption/premise verification). I probed every codebase assertion in the plan via Read/Grep rather than relying on cached knowledge.

AFFIRMATIONS (assertions verified true against source):
- "`apply_outbound.py:416,441`" — VERIFIED: `is_not_found(exc)` at apply_outbound.py:416 and `getattr(exc, "status_code", None) in (404, 410, 403)` at :441, both inside `except JiraAPIError as exc:` blocks.
- "`batch_dispatch.py:77,307`" — VERIFIED: `exc.status_code` 4xx fast-fail at batch_dispatch.py:77 (in `except JiraAPIError`), and `http_status(exc)` at :307 (`_is_illegal_transition_400`).
- "the `_engine/rebar_reconciler/_errors.py` helpers already exist" — VERIFIED: `_errors.py:6 http_status(...)` and `:22 is_not_found(...)` are present and implemented.
- "`reconcile.py:1155` `except BaseException ... raise`" — VERIFIED verbatim: reconcile.py:1155 `except BaseException as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — must re-raise after recording` then `raise` at :1157.
- "Migrate stderr-diagnostic prints (`reconcile.py`, `__main__.py`, etc.)" — VERIFIED such prints exist (reconcile.py has ~20 print sites; __main__.py has stderr `ERROR:`/`OK:` prints), and __main__.py also emits stdout data output (report at :162, json result at :254), corroborating the "keep acli/jira data output semantics intact" caveat.

FINDINGS:

- [ ] [E4] AC4 asserts "This package removed from the allowlist", but the ONLY current allowlist entry for this package is a `T201` per-file-ignore that pyproject.toml documents as a PERMANENT legitimate-print surface, not a removable transitional entry — verify which allowlist AC4 means and correct the wording.
  criteria: [E4]
  location: Acceptance Criteria line 4 ("This package removed from the allowlist") vs pyproject.toml:202-232
  evidence:
    - pyproject.toml:226-231 (the only per-file-ignore touching this package's BLE001 history) reads: "reconciler subprocess — BLE001 SWEPT by 0569 ... KEEPS T201: the reconciler is a subprocess whose stderr ... + stdout ... prints ARE its operational output — a legitimate-print surface."
    - pyproject.toml:232 `"src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*" = ["T201"]` — a KEPT T201 ignore, with no BLE001 entry present for this package. There is no remaining BLE001 allowlist line to remove; the T201 line is explicitly NOT slated for removal.
    - The plan body's own caveats ("Migrate stderr-diagnostic prints ... keep any acli/jira data output semantics intact"; rollback/cleanup swallows "stay intentionally broad") are consistent with T201 staying — so "removed from the allowlist" is ambiguous/likely inaccurate as written.
  suggested_fix: Reword AC4 to name the specific removal precisely — e.g. "the BLE001 ignore for this package is removed (no BLE001 per-file-ignore line for src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/ remains); the T201 entry at pyproject.toml:232 stays as a documented legitimate-print surface" — so the criterion is verifiable.

- [ ] [E4] The "add characterization tests FIRST" premise treats the safety net as not-yet-existing, but `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_errors_classification.py` already pins `is_not_found`/`http_status` and is explicitly tagged to this ticket — clarify whether the AC is "author new" or "confirm/extend existing".
  criteria: [E4]
  location: "What" bullet 1 + AC1 ("Characterization tests cover ... before remediation") vs tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_errors_classification.py:1-15
  evidence:
    - test_errors_classification.py:1-8 docstring: "Characterization tests for the reconciler's HTTP error-classification helpers. Pins the CURRENT behavior of `http_status` / `is_not_found` BEFORE the error-handling sweep (epic ring-gun-jot, ticket 0569) touches the broad `except` sites..." — i.e. this file is named as 0569's own safety net.
    - test_errors_classification.py:15 `from rebar_reconciler._errors import http_status, is_not_found` and helper exception classes (`_StatusCodeExc`, `_CodeExc`, `_BothExc`) exercise both `.status_code` and `.code` paths — the classification is already characterized.
  suggested_fix: State in the plan whether AC1 is satisfied by extending the existing test_errors_classification.py (and to what coverage delta) or by adding new tests, so a reviewer can verify the "FIRST" ordering claim against an already-present file rather than assuming it is absent.

Note: both findings are clarity/verifiability of stated premises, not blockers — the underlying remediation scope is well-grounded and the file/line references all check out. (Separate from the G4 logging-substrate findings already on this ticket, which I did not re-litigate.)

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

Characterization tests for is_not_found/http_status added FIRST (6 tests, pin status_code-over-code precedence, non-int->None, only-404). All 20 broad-excepts to floor with justified noqa, ZERO behavior change: 8 cleanup-and-reraise (incl. 3 BaseException kept since they re-raise), 4 rollback-must-not-mask-original, 8 fail-open. Reconciler unit suite 977 pass; full suite 3215 passed/13 skipped run ALONE (serialized), HEAD stable. make lint green.

Completion verification: **PASS** — all four acceptance criteria are met.

**AC1 — Characterization tests cover `is_not_found`/`http_status` classification before remediation.**

content.text: ## What
The test repo-isolation guard `_no_repo_root_leaks` (`tests/conftest.py:157-181`)
only diffs the **top-level** entries of `REPO_ROOT` (`set(os.listdir(_REPO_ROOT))`
before vs after each test). It is therefore **blind to a test leaking state INTO a
pre-existing top-level directory** — most importantly `.rebar/` (this checkout
dogfoods rebar, so `.rebar/` always exists locally). A test that writes
`.rebar/run_snapshots/<sha>` adds no *new* top-level entry, so the guard passes
locally — while a fresh CI checkout (no `.rebar/`) sees the SAME write *create*
`.rebar` and flags it. This is a false-green/false-red split between local and CI.
## Why
This is the exact gap that hid the `test_sweep_removes_readonly_snapshot_tree` leak
(fixed in `fc792e4f`): the test snapshotted into `cwd = REPO_ROOT`'s `.rebar/`, which
was invisible to the top-level guard locally but failed CI's clean checkout. The
guard's job is to catch tests that escape their sandbox; right now it catches only
the subset that create a *new top-level* entry, giving false confidence locally.
## Reproduction Steps
1. Ensure `REPO_ROOT/.rebar/` exists (it does in any checkout that has run rebar).
2. Add a test that writes a file under `REPO_ROOT/.rebar/` (e.g. `(_REPO_ROOT /
".rebar" / "leaked.txt").write_text("x")`) without using `tmp_path`.
3. Run it **locally**: the `_no_repo_root_leaks` guard does NOT fail it (the new file
is inside the pre-existing `.rebar/`, so the top-level `os.listdir` diff is empty).
4. Run it on a **fresh checkout** (no `.rebar/`): the write creates `.rebar/` → the
top-level diff flags `['.rebar']` → the test fails.
**Expected:** a test that writes anywhere under `REPO_ROOT` it didn't create is flagged
**locally**, regardless of whether the containing dir pre-existed.
**Actual:** leaks into a pre-existing top-level dir (`.rebar/`, etc.) pass locally and
only surface (if at all) on a clean CI checkout.
## Proposed improvement
Extend the guard to detect new entries created **inside** the volatile pre-existing
dirs, not just at the top level — while staying cheap and not flagging dirs with
legitimate per-test churn:
- Snapshot a deeper path set for the known cache/state dirs that tests must NOT touch
in `REPO_ROOT` — at minimum `.rebar/` (one level: `{f".rebar/{e}" for e in
os.listdir(REPO_ROOT/'.rebar')}`), diffed before/after alongside the top-level set.
Optionally generalize to a bounded recursive walk that **excludes** the dirs with
legitimate churn (`.git/`, `.tickets-tracker/`, `.venv/`, caches) so it stays fast.
- Keep the existing best-effort cleanup of leaked paths, extended to the deeper set.
- (Belt-and-suspenders, optional) add a CI/local parity note or a `make` target that
runs a representative slice in a throwaway checkout, so the local↔CI environment gap
(pre-existing `.rebar`, installed extras, filesystem) is exercised before push.
The underlying test bug is already fixed; this ticket hardens the *guard* so the next
such leak fails locally instead of only in CI.
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `_no_repo_root_leaks` flags a test that creates a new entry under a pre-existing
`REPO_ROOT/.rebar/` (verified by a self-test that deliberately leaks into `.rebar/`
and asserts the guard fails it) — even when `.rebar/` pre-exists.
- [ ] The guard still passes the full existing suite (no false positives from dirs with
legitimate per-test churn — `.git/`, `.tickets-tracker/`, etc. remain excluded).
- [ ] The check stays inexpensive (no full-repo recursive walk of large dirs; scoped to
the known state dirs or a bounded/excluded walk).
- [ ] Leaked deeper paths are cleaned up (best-effort) as the top-level ones already are.
</content>

content.text: ## What

The 0ac6 slice-3 review found a 10th JIRA_* consumer NOT routed through the typed Config: `src/rebar/_engine_support/bridge_fsck.py` (enumerate_open_count_skew_anomalies, ~lines 279-291) still reads JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_PROJECT/JIRA_API_TOKEN directly from os.environ when constructing an AcliClient. It is OUTSIDE the rebar_reconciler package (_engine_support) and its own comment notes it is NOT on any live path, so it was deliberately left out of slice 3's enumerated 9 reconciler sites.
Consequence: a [tool.rebar.jira] config-file value is consumed everywhere in the reconciler but NOT by this bridge_fsck anomaly helper — a minor consistency gap.
## Scope / consideration
- Route the url/user/project through the typed Config (env JIRA_URL/USER/PROJECT override file); keep the secret JIRA_API_TOKEN env-only.

- NOTE the cross-package wrinkle: bridge_fsck is in _engine_support and resolve_jira_settings lives in the reconciler floor (rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess). Either (a) import the reconciler floor, or (b) resolve inline via `from rebar.config import load_config` (no reconciler import) — prefer whichever keeps _engine_support decoupled from the reconciler package.
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] bridge_fsck's AcliClient url/user/project come from load_config().jira.* (env overrides file); JIRA_API_TOKEN stays env-only.
- [ ] A test asserts a [tool.rebar.jira] file value reaches that consumer (or, if it stays env-only by design, document why).
- [ ] No new _engine_support -> rebar_reconciler import coupling unless justified.

content.text:
PROGRESS — field validation probe fully de-staled + corrected + green on baseline AND HEAD.

Fixed (commit d8c1bda9):
1. CLI drift: 23 'rebar ticket <verb>' calls -> top-level 'rebar <verb>' across both probes (subagent confirmed zero other occurrences repo-wide).
2. macOS mktemp: trailing-XXXXXX fix.
3. Inbound assertions rewritten to the documented conflict_resolver FIELD_CLASSES policy: state (title/priority/status/assignee/type)=local-wins; additive (description/comments)=merge/local-first; set (labels)=union add-only. Phase 3a repurposed to a genuine inbound positive control (set-union label ADD lands on untouched ticket) + negative control (state title stays local-wins).
4. Phase 6a: reworked from false 1-pass-convergence to EVENTUAL idempotency (reconciler settles a Jira edit over 2-3 passes; still fails real non-convergence).
5. Added a clear FIELD SYNC MATRIX (per-field Class + Create/Update-out/Update-in + legend) documenting what is/isn't synced, with CRUD coverage.

Root cause of the original inbound failures was confirmed by a controlled live test: the reconciler pushes local->Jira and suppresses inbound for STATE fields (resolve_state returns local unconditionally) — i.e. the probe's expectations were wrong, not the code. Identical on baseline and HEAD (move-only split).

Live DIG result: 167 passed / 0 failed on the pre-split baseline (19f6dea5) AND on HEAD — identical parity. This is the full-pipeline confirmation that the acli split preserved Jira-sync behavior.

REMAINING: e2e_validation_probe.sh (lighter companion) got the de-stale fix but runs UNFILTERED bootstrap-strict; in a store with local-only tickets that is unsafe (would push them to DIG) + non-idempotent. It needs --filter-local-ids scoping (like the field probe) to be safe/green here — pending decision.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

COMPLETED — both jira-pressure-test probes fully de-staled, corrected, and green on baseline AND HEAD with identical parity.

Commits: d8c1bda9 (field probe), e6283484 (lighter probe).

Field probe (e2e_field_validation_probe.sh): 167 passed / 0 failed on baseline (19f6dea5) and HEAD. Fixes: CLI ticket-prefix, mktemp BSD portability, all inbound assertions rewritten to the documented conflict_resolver FIELD_CLASSES policy (state=local-wins, additive=merge, set=union-add-only), Phase 3a positive+negative inbound controls, Phase 6a eventual-idempotency, and a clear FIELD SYNC MATRIX (per-field Class + Create/Update-out/Update-in + legend) with CRUD coverage.

Lighter probe (e2e_validation_probe.sh): 25 passed / 0 failed on baseline and HEAD. Fixes: CLI ticket-prefix, --filter-local-ids scoping (was unsafe unfiltered bootstrap-strict), awk mutation-count (BSD grep -P removed), CLI-based local edit (was direct ticket.json file write — store is event-sourced), settle-then-assert idempotency.

All root causes were pre-existing staleness (CLI drift, macOS portability, event-sourcing migration, eventual-consistency) — none related to the acli split, which both probes confirm preserved Jira-sync behavior through the full reconcile pipeline (identical baseline=HEAD). No DIG/local orphans left.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

The reference probes in `scripts/jira-pressure-test/` (e2e_field_validation_probe.sh, e2e_validation_probe.sh) call the rebar CLI as `\"\$TICKET_CLI\" ticket <verb>`, but the current CLI has no `ticket` subcommand group — verbs are top-level (`rebar create`, `edit`, `comment`, `tag`, `untag`, `show`, `transition`, `delete`). The probes abort at the first call (`unknown subcommand 'ticket'`). Surfaced while closing tangly-abbey-smelt (the acli split): these probes were the intended live-DIG parity gate but were unrunnable due to this drift.

content.text: ## Summary

Sub-effort (c) of story fatty-cipher-range. The event log is the wire format between clones running DIFFERENT rebar versions; declare it versioned and pin forward compatibility for unknown event types.
## Semantics decision (recorded — resolves the "preserved-and-ignored" ambiguity)
- "Ignored": replay skips unknown event_type values WITHOUT error (this is already the behavior — _processors.py:414 — pin it with a test, including that the ticket remains fully readable).

- "Preserved" means at the FILE level, not state level: unknown-type event FILES must survive untouched. The critical case is COMPACTION: rebar compact must NOT absorb or delete unknown-type events into a SNAPSHOT (else a newer clone's data is destroyed by an older clone's compaction). Verify what ticket-compact.sh does with unknown types today; if it absorbs/deletes them, THAT is the code fix in this ticket.
- NO new VERSION event type, no version negotiation. SCHEMA_VERSION constant in ticket_reducer (new _version.py or __init__) + version declaration in docs/event-schema.md.
## Suggested RED test

tests/interfaces/test_event_schema_forward_compat.py: write an event with event_type=FUTURE_TYPE into a ticket dir -> reduce_ticket succeeds, state readable; then run compaction over that ticket -> the FUTURE_TYPE event file still exists on disk afterward and replay still succeeds. RED if compaction destroys it.
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SCHEMA_VERSION constant + versioned docs/event-schema.md
- [ ] Unknown-type replay tolerance pinned by test
- [ ] Compaction proven (or fixed) to preserve unknown-type event files

### High-confidence advisories (folded into this ticket's description where actionable):
- [0.86 major] The plan relies on tracker_dir()'s return-value contract without stating it, yet uses the result both as a full path (os.path.join(repo, ...) at line 152) and a
- [0.71 major] Migration verifies replacement but not removal: the criteria never assert that NO hardcoded '.tickets-tracker' literals remain in init.py. The 'What' lists 4 sp
- [0.71 major] No test or verification approach is described for either the 'byte-for-byte unchanged' default invariant or the new custom-dir behavior. This is not a pure rena
- [0.71 major] The plan does not address existing repositories already initialized with '.tickets-tracker' when tracker.dir is later changed, nor cleanup of the old symlink/ex
- [0.71 major] The plan does not address the transition / retirement path when a repo already initialized with the default '.tickets-tracker' dir+symlink later has a custom tr
- [0.71 critical] The plan creates and symlinks a custom tracker dir from init, but the epic notes ~20 hardcoded call sites and this child migrates only init's creation/symlink/e

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->

Implemented: tracker path/symlink/exclude in init.py routed through config.tracker_dir(); exclude entry is now the repo-relative tracker path (handles custom + nested + absolute-relocation dirs). Smoke-tested: a custom tracker.dir='my-ticket-store' creates+excludes+round-trips create/list. 26 init tests pass; subagent review clean (fixed a stale docstring). Default .tickets-tracker behavior byte-for-byte unchanged.

<!-- rebar:reconciler-echo -->
## What
Replace hardcoded `.tickets-tracker` worktree/symlink-dir literals with `tracker_dir()`
(or its basename) so a custom dir is created, symlinked, and gitignored correctly.
Call sites in `src/rebar/_commands/init.py`:
- line 152: dir creation `os.path.join(repo, ".tickets-tracker")`.
- line 199: the git `_exclude(...)` it writes — must use the resolved basename so a custom
dir is auto-gitignored.
- line 418: the worktree-path resolver (`.../<main worktree>/.tickets-tracker`).
- lines 461-469: symlink name at the repo root.
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `init` uses `tracker_dir()`/its basename for dir creation, symlink, and exclude.
- [ ] A custom `tracker.dir` is created, symlinked at the repo root, and added to git excludes.
- [ ] Default `.tickets-tracker` behavior is byte-for-byte unchanged.
## Files
- `src/rebar/_commands/init.py`
## Depends on
- Child 2 (canonical `tracker_dir()` resolver).
## Review refinements (plan-review gate)
- E1 (conf 0.71): assert REMOVAL, not just replacement — no hardcoded `.tickets-tracker`
string literals remain in init's migrated paths after the change (grep-style assertion).
- [ ] A check/test asserts zero residual `.tickets-tracker` literals in the migrated init paths.

REB-98.summary: migrate worktree-dir call sites in init (creation/symlink/exclude) to tracker_dir()

content.text: ## Context
The plan-review gate (`5fd2`) deliberately EXEMPTS bugs in v1 — a bug has a different shape (reproduction,
expected-vs-actual, fix-plan) than a feature plan, and round-4 data showed the plan criteria draw mostly
planning-noise on bugs. This epic designs the dedicated bug-review gate. (Spun out of `5fd2`; was follow-on F6.)
## Success Criteria
- A review gate tuned to bug tickets (reproduction adequacy, root-cause vs symptom, fix-plan soundness,
regression-test presence), reusing the three-pass structure and the shared infrastructure.
- Bugs are no longer simply skipped: they get an appropriate, low-false-positive review.
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A bug-specific criteria set (reproduction, expected-vs-actual, root-cause, regression test) on the three-pass structure.
- [ ] Validated on real bug tickets across the corpuses; low false-positive vs the plan criteria on bugs.
- [ ] Reuses the shared review infrastructure (engine, Pass-2/Pass-3, eval seam).

REB-99.summary: Bug-ticket review gate (dedicated; the plan-review gate exempts bugs in v1)
